This study focuses on using ibuprofen as a model drug to develop a novel wound dressing or tissue engineering scaffold capable of **prolonged sustained drug release without dangerous “sudden release”**. This is outside the scope of our study.

This paper uses Ibuprofen as a model drug to explore a critical issue in the pharmaceutical industry: In vitro experiments, which type of “artificial skin” most accurately mimics real human skin? In this study, ibuprofen, acting as an anion, was mixed with lidocaine (a cation) to form an ionic liquid (IL). Instead of using animal skin, the study employed a silicone membrane as a model, with the sole purpose of analyzing the diffusion behavior of the ionic liquid during transmembrane transport from the perspectives of mathematical equations and thermodynamic activity. The experiments tested mixtures with different molar ratios. The molar fraction of ibuprofen (X_{Ibu}) was set to multiple values ranging from 0 to 1.0 (e.g., 0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 0.9, etc.).
